    "thinking": " I'm noticing the user probably meant v1.0 rather than v10.0 based on the version history, and they want performance improvements alongside setting SCALE to 1.75 as default. At that scale, we're looking at around 1.15 million cells with multiple simulation passes per step, so I need to identify where the biggest bottlenecks are in the rendering and computation pipeline.\n\nI've already made substantial optimizations in v0.7 \u2014 idle skipping, fused pigment operations, and box blur improvements \u2014 so I'm thinking about what's still left on the table. Active-region tracking seems promising...\n\nwhen someone paints a small stroke on a large canvas, the simulation runs everywhere even though most cells are dormant. If I tracked a bounding box around where action is happening and only simulated within that region (plus some margin for diffusion), I could skip 98% of the cells at typical scales. It's a big potential win but requires refactoring most of the simulation passes since they currently iterate over the full grid dimensions. I'm also noticing that wet-skipping already exists in some passes like updateVelocity and movePigment, so extending that pattern elsewhere might help too.\n\nLooking at where time is spent: most passes already gate on wet values, but evaporate is an unconditional loop over all cells. The evaporate function itself is extremely cheap though\u2014just a multiply, compare, and write per cell. It's probably not the bottleneck.\n\nThe real cost is in the box blur for edge darkening, which runs two separate passes over the entire grid every frame even when only a small region has wet pixels. Active-region tracking could help, but localizing the box blur is tricky because of the running-sum state it maintains.\n\nRendering is also significant\u2014compositing three pigments across 750K cells comes to roughly 22 million operations per frame, and there might be room to optimize that path.\n\nI'm thinking through how to implement active-region tracking more carefully. The idea is to maintain a bounding box around all cells with non-zero wet or pigment values, expand it when the user paints, and optionally shrink it at the edges each frame if they've dried out. Then all the simulation loops would stay within that box plus a small margin for diffusion, which should reduce the work considerably without too much added complexity.\n\nThe main risk is that the bounding box needs to be conservative enough to catch diffusion and advection fronts, otherwise cells outside the box that should be simulated will get skipped. For the box blur in applyEdgeDarkening, the running-sum algorithm is trickier since it needs to process columns sequentially, so I'd either need to reinitialize the running sum at the active region's left edge or find another approach to restrict which rows get processed.\n\nActually, the box blur isn't worth optimizing since it's fundamentally O(N) across the full width anyway. The idle-skip optimization already handles the case where there are no wet cells, so that's covered. I'm thinking about skipping the render pass entirely when nothing visible has changed between frames\u2014like during very slow drying phases\u2014but I need to figure out how to detect that efficiently.\n\nWait, I already have that: the render only runs if the sim is active or if fade is happening. When the canvas is completely idle and static, render doesn't get called at all. That's good.\n\nI could try detecting cells with only one non-zero pigment and skip processing the other two in transferPigment, but the per-cell branching might hurt more than it helps due to branch prediction overhead. Bigger architectural changes like SIMD via WebAssembly or WebGPU would be overkill for this. I could also try using smaller data types like Uint16 for the wet array to cut memory bandwidth in half, but that requires careful arithmetic and feels risky.\n\nReducing simSteps from 2 to 1 per frame would halve the computational cost, but the pigment and water dynamics would evolve half as fast, creating a visible slowdown. I could try combining it with a smaller timestep to maintain the same dynamics, but the simulation uses 2 steps specifically because of stability constraints\u2014I can't just merge them without breaking that.\n\nI'm looking at expanding the early-exit conditions in diffuseWet to skip more cases when all five cells in a neighborhood meet certain criteria.  Similar skips could apply to updateVelocity when wet is zero, and transferPigment when wet, pigment, and dye are all zero. The existing gate at wet < 0.04 catches most inactive cells, but dry pigment cells with no water still get processed unnecessarily.\n\nI'm thinking about defining tighter active regions for each pass\u2014diffuseWet would only touch cells with water or neighbors with water, updateVelocity would skip below the 0.04 threshold, movePigment would require both water and pigment present, and transferPigment would have its own criteria.\n\nBefore diving into implementation though, I should actually measure the current performance. The user might be seeing frame drops at higher scales, or they're just being proactive about optimization for the v1.0 release. Active region tracking seems like the highest-impact approach, so let me start prototyping that.\n\nImplementing active region tracking... Setting up the active box management functions to track which regions need simulation updates\u2014expanding the bounds when paint events occur, resetting to full canvas when the simulation or rewetting happens, then iterating through only the active region during each sim pass to optimize performance.\n\nMost passes can use the active box, but edge darkening with box blur is tricky since the kernel extends past the region boundaries and needs valid input from outside cells, and rendering still requires processing the full grid to display all visible pigment. Rather than over-engineer the blur localization right now, I should focus on simpler optimizations that'll have more impact.\n\nThe core simulation passes\u2014diffuse, velocity, pigment movement, and transfer\u2014all iterate the full grid with per-cell gates, so switching to bounding box iteration would eliminate overhead even for cells that would've been skipped anyway. With a 200px stroke on a 750K-cell grid at scale 1.75, the savings could be substantial.\n\nThe real challenge is maintaining the bounding box as the simulation evolves. When wet areas dry out through evaporation, the box should shrink back down. I could periodically scan the bbox edges to detect when rows or columns have no active cells, or tie the shrinking logic into the existing idle-detection system.\n\nActually, the cleanest approach is to reset the bbox when the canvas goes idle\u2014since the simulation isn't running anyway, I can just rescan for active regions when it wakes up, or let the next paint operation establish a fresh bbox. During continuous painting, the bbox naturally grows with the painted area, which is fine. The wet diffusion halo extends a few cells beyond the brush, so a 4-cell margin handles that. I also need to reset the bbox in resetSim() to keep things clean.\n\nBut here's the thing: after resetSim(), the whole canvas is uniformly wet, so technically all cells would be \"active\" by the wet threshold. However, there's no pigment and no pressure anywhere, so the actual computational work is minimal even with a full-grid bbox. Maybe I should refine the definition of \"active\" to mean cells with actual pressure or pigment, rather than just meeting the wet threshold.\n\nLooking at what each sim step actually does, they have different requirements: diffuseWet needs wet gradients, updateVelocity needs pressure or wet gradients with sufficient wetness, movePigment only matters where there's pigment, transferPigment requires the wet threshold, and evaporate just needs any wet. So the bbox should really track which cells actually need updates based on these varying conditions. passes have different active regions. The largest is \"wet > 0\" (for evaporate and diffuseWet). Others need wet >= 0.04 AND something else.\n\nFor simplicity, let me use one bbox that covers \"wet > 0 OR pressure > 0 OR pigment > 0\" \u2014 any cell that needs ANY sim work. Per pass, the per-cell gates filter further.\n\nWhen canvas is in initial wet state (post-resetSim), bbox is full canvas, but per-cell work is minimal (no pressure, no pigment).\n\nWhen painting begins, bbox expands to include painted region.\n\nOver time, painted region with wet > 0 might expand (diffusion). Bbox should track.\n\nWhen wet evaporates everywhere except some painted pigment regions, bbox should shrink. But there's the pigment-only cells that still need transferPigment etc. So bbox can't shrink too aggressively.\n\nI think the simplest approach is to track the bounding box of all non-default state \u2014 initially the full canvas when fully wet, then expanding to encompass painted regions. Rather than shrinking dynamically, I'll keep it stable during active simulation and only reset it when the canvas is rewet or cleared. This means early sessions with localized painting get good savings, but a long session with scattered strokes eventually covers most of the canvas.\n\nFor the performance math, I'm looking at the actual cost per frame: with 720 simulation passes per second and 700K cells failing the gate check in a localized painting scenario, that's a lot of branch operations. Even just the gate check itself adds up across all those passes.\n\nActive region tracking cuts that down significantly\u2014reducing gate checks by 14x means I'm saving about 9% of a single core's capacity. But the real win is probably the cache efficiency from iterating over a much smaller region, which could give an indirect speedup beyond just the iteration reduction.\n\nThis seems worth implementing. Though I'm noticing there might be an even simpler optimization I should tackle first\u2014something in the `updateVelocity` function that handles pressure decay.\n\nChecking the actual implementation...",

Since paperH is already filled and state arrays are zeroed, render will iterate the full canvas and produce the paper texture with speckle, which is exactly what we want.\n\nOnce resetSim completes, the active region is empty, so the sim loop runs but does nothing visible until idle detection kicks in. When the user paints, markCanvasActive resumes the sim and expands the active region to include the brush stroke.\n\nI'm realizing that paintAt deposits pigment directly into the grid arrays, so cells with pigment will be processed correctly once the active region is set. The plan checks out.\n\nFor applyEdgeDarkening, I'm keeping it as a full-grid operation for now since box blur is hard to localize, and the per-cell cost is small. With idle-skip handling, this overhead is acceptable. Though I'm noticing that when simStep runs during painting, applyEdgeDarkening processes the entire grid even though only the active region matters. I need to think through whether the box blur can be constrained to avoid wasting cycles on cells outside the active region.\n\nActually, looking at how this works: wetBinary gets set to 0/1 based on whether wet exceeds 0.04, so cells outside the active region naturally have wetBinary=0. The box blur spreads this, creating small non-zero values at the edges, but the apply step gates on wet > 0.04, so most cells outside the active region skip the actual work anyway. The three O(N) passes are cheap enough that the gating handles the real cost.\n\nRather than localizing applyEdgeDarkening (which would be complex because of the box blur), I'll focus on the loops that are easier to constrain. I need to clamp diffuseWet to the active region bounds and do the same for updateVelocity's main loop, then swap the velocity arrays only within that region.\n\nFor the remaining functions\u2014movePigment, transferPigment, evaporate, and render\u2014I'll apply the same active region clamping to their iteration bounds. updateBars stays full-N since I'll handle performance through throttling instead.\n\nOne thing to verify: wetBinary, wetBlur, and wetBlurLarge all get rewritten fresh on each applyEdgeDarkening call, so even if the active region expands, those cached values won't cause stale data issues.\n\nNow for u_new and v_new\u2014after the pointer swap in updateVelocity, the array that was u_new becomes the new u, so cells inside the active region get updated values while cells outside retain whatever they had before. Since those outside cells should be zero (they're dry), and the initial Float32Array allocation zero-inits them, I need to make sure the dry-cell branch in updateVelocity properly zeroes u_new for cells outside the active region.\n\nActually, I'm realizing there's a difference between copying with `.set()` versus swapping pointers. With `.set()`, both u and u_new end up with the same values. But with a pointer swap, u_new becomes the old array, which means on the next iteration I'd be writing to what used to be u\u2014so I need to make sure I'm not accidentally reusing stale data from cells that were previously outside the active region. After swap, u is what was u_new (new values), and u_new is the OLD u (old values from before this simStep).\n\nFor the next simStep, updateVelocity reads u to compute new velocities. It reads from `u[i]` \u2014 now this is the new value (correct). It writes to `u_new[i]` \u2014 now writing into the OLD u array (which gets overwritten anyway). \u2713\n\nBut wait \u2014 cells OUTSIDE the active region. updateVelocity doesn't iterate them. Their values in BOTH arrays stay the same. After swap, u contains... whatever u_new had before. Which is whatever was written when this cell was last in the active region. Plus, in cells that are outside the new active region but were inside before, u_new might have stale values.\n\nLet me trace through what happens to a cell that was active in a previous step but is now outside the active region. That old step wrote some value (probably 0) to u_new, and after the swap it ended up in u. Now in the current step, updateVelocity skips this cell entirely, so both u and u_new keep their old values. After the next swap, they just flip back and forth between the same two stale values.\n\nThe real issue is that evaporate only decrements the wet count but doesn't clear the velocity arrays, so cells that just dried out could still have non-zero u and v values lingering in them.\n\nIn the old code, updateVelocity processed every cell each iteration, so dry cells would immediately get zeroed out. But with the active region optimization, dry cells outside the active region never get processed, meaning their stale velocities persist. However, this shouldn't matter since those cells are outside the active region and nothing reads their velocities anyway.\n\nThe tricky part is when a cell inside the active region needs to compute its velocity using neighbors\u2014if a neighbor is outside the active region, I need to figure out what velocity value gets used for that neighbor in the calculation.\n\nSince the active region only grows and never shrinks, cells just outside it have never been processed in this session, so their velocities should still be at the initial zero value. And cells within the margin around the active region boundary are typically dry (wet ~ 0), so their velocities would be zero anyway from previous dry-cell handling.\n\nLet me think through what happens during a reset... Cells outside any painted region stay at zero since they never entered the active region post-reset, and cells inside the active region get their velocities computed and updated as the region expands. The pointer swap is safe as long as the active region only grows. I need to make sure the margin gets applied correctly\u2014I'll compute the brush footprint in paintAt, then call activateRegion with those bounds so the margin gets added inside that function.\n\nDefining activateRegion with margin handling... The empty bounding box is detected when activeX1 is less than activeX0, which naturally prevents the simulation loops from iterating. Now I'm laying out the implementation steps: bumping the scale default to 1.75, updating the version to 1.0, adding the active region state variables and helper functions, then integrating the activation logic into the paint function.\n\nFor the reset flow, I need to ensure the active region covers the full canvas when the initial render happens after resetSim, since control returns to the main loop which will trigger the render on the next iteration. full canvas. But subsequent renders also do full canvas (since active region never shrinks)... which we don't want.\n\nSo active region needs to shrink back down after that initial render. The explicit approach is to set the active region to full before rendering, call render directly to draw everything, then reset the active region to empty so future renders only update what's changed.\n\nInitializing the simulation state...\n\nLet me verify the function order \u2014 render is defined at line 1071, resetSim at line 1286, and resetSim gets called at line 2090, so render will definitely be available when I need it. The plan should work. I'm settling on having render() always use the active region, so resetSim will expand it to full bounds before rendering and then reset it back. For rewet, I need to think through how to handle the display update when wet values change and particles lift from the ground layer to the gas layer \u2014 I want the active region to track only cells that actually changed. non-zero pigment? Or stay full?\n\nAfter rewet, most cells have wet=0.85 but pigment is still localized to painted areas. The painted areas had pigment in g (some), now have more in g (after lift). Sim needs to process pigment movement everywhere that has pigment.\n\nHmm. The active region should include all painted cells. Resetting to empty after rewet would lose this.\n\nMaybe: rewet should NOT reset active region. Just set fullActiveRegion before render, then... what?\n\nActually, if active region was already tracking all painted areas, it covers them. rewet doesn't add new painted areas; it just lifts existing pigment. So existing active region is fine.\n\nBut rewet also sets wet across the whole canvas. The wet diffuses, etc. The active region only covers painted areas. Wet outside the active region doesn't diffuse properly.\n\nI think the simplest approach is to just have rewet set the active region to full and leave it there. That way wet can diffuse everywhere it needs to, and I don't have to worry about the diffusion logic breaking outside the tracked region. Yeah, performance will degrade in long sessions with rewet, but rewet is rare enough that it's not worth the complexity. The active region only grows, so once a cell is painted it stays in the region and gets rendered every frame\u2014no risk of stale pixels. If the user paints in a different area, the bounding box expands to cover both regions and everything between them, which also gets rendered correctly. Now I'm ready to implement, starting with adding the active region state variables and the margin constant, then defining the functions that manage them.\n\nModifying the existing functions to use these bounds: paintAt will activate the region at the start, resetSim will fully activate and render before resetting, rewet will fully activate at the end, and the diffusion, velocity, pigment movement, and transfer functions will all swap their loop bounds to respect the active region instead of iterating over the full canvas.\n\nI also need to update evaporate and render with the same loop bound pattern, throttle updateBars to run every 6 frames instead of every frame, adjust the default slider value from 2 to 1.75, and bump the version number from v0.9 to v1.0.\n\nFor the functions that currently iterate through all N cells with a simple index loop, I'm converting them to 2D iteration over the active region so they only process the cells that matter. The pressure decay in updateVelocity needs the same treatment. Now let me start implementing these changes, though I should make sure to preserve the wet buffer copy in diffuseWet.\n\nActually, I'm reconsidering whether to copy the entire wet array or just the active region. The full array copy using `.set()` is extremely fast since it's essentially a memcpy operation, but if the active region is much smaller than the full grid, copying only that region might be more efficient despite the loop overhead.\n\nFor typical stroke sizes in the 50-500K cell range, the memcpy speed of `.set()` probably still wins out. I'll stick with the current approach for now and revisit if profiling shows it's a bottleneck.\n\nThough I'm noticing the same issue exists in movePigment's diffusion step, where it copies all three pigment arrays in full for every simulation step, even though the actual diffusion loop is localized to the active region.\n\nI could optimize those `.set()` calls to only copy the active region, but for now I'll keep them full-N since the real performance gains come from localizing the expensive inner loops.\n\nAlright, time to actually implement this. Scans the current rect for cells above the\\n//     activity threshold and tightens to that bounding box + margin.\\n//     Lets the rect tighten as pigment dries / fades / lifts.\\n//\\n// Empty rect (activeMaxX < activeMinX, i.e. -1): simStep functions\\n// early-return. This is the initial state (resetSim leaves uniform\\n// wet but no pigment/pressure) and the post-dry state.\\nlet activeMinX = 0, activeMaxX = -1;  // -1 marker = empty\\nlet activeMinY = 0, activeMaxY = -1;\\nlet framesSinceShrink = 0;\\nconst ACTIVE_SHRINK_INTERVAL = 30;    // frames between shrink scans\\nconst ACTIVE_MARGIN = 24;             // cells of padding around tracked content\\nconst ACTIVE_THRESHOLD = 0.001;       // pigment/pressure threshold for \\\"active\\\"\\n\\nfunction activeRectIsEmpty() {\\n  return activeMaxX < activeMinX || activeMaxY < activeMinY;\\n}\\n\\nfunction setActiveRectFull() {\\n  activeMinX = 0; activeMaxX = GW - 1;\\n  activeMinY = 0; activeMaxY = GH - 1;\\n}\\n\\nfunction setActiveRectEmpty() {\\n  activeMinX = 0; activeMaxX = -1;\\n  activeMinY = 0; activeMaxY = -1;\\n}\\n\\nfunction expandActiveRect(centerX, centerY, radius) {\\n  const lx = Math.max(0, Math.floor(centerX - radius - ACTIVE_MARGIN));\\n  const rx = Math.min(GW - 1, Math.ceil(centerX + radius + ACTIVE_MARGIN));\\n  const ty = Math.max(0, Math.floor(centerY - radius - ACTIVE_MARGIN));\\n  const by = Math.min(GH - 1, Math.ceil(centerY + radius + ACTIVE_MARGIN));\\n  if (activeRectIsEmpty()) {\\n    activeMinX = lx; activeMaxX = rx;\\n    activeMinY = ty; activeMaxY = by;\\n  } else {\\n    if (lx < activeMinX) activeMinX = lx;\\n    if (rx > activeMaxX) activeMaxX = rx;\\n    if (ty < activeMinY) activeMinY = ty;\\n    if (by > activeMaxY) activeMaxY = by;\\n  }\\n}\\n\\nfunction shrinkActiveRect() {\\n  if (activeRectIsEmpty()) return;\\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\\n  const thr = ACTIVE_THRESHOLD;\\n  let newMinX = activeMaxX + 1, newMaxX = activeMinX - 1;\\n  let newMinY = activeMaxY + 1, newMaxY = activeMinY - 1;\\n  for (let y = activeMinY; When a cell exits the active region, it keeps whatever velocity it had when it was last updated, which is the same behavior as the explicit copy approach. The key difference is that with pointer swap, u_new for the next step starts with the previous step's u values, which means cells that were never active still carry their initial values forward.\n\nPointer swap is vastly faster than per-row subarray copies \u2014 just two pointer reassignments versus W\u00d7H float copies \u2014 but there's a subtle issue I'm noticing about how the old values propagate.\n\nWhen the active region grows between steps, newly active cells read from u (which was swapped from the previous u_new), creating a chain of values through multiple swaps.     "thinking": " I'm realizing that toggling the transparent background only affects the CSS styling, not the actual canvas rendering, so it doesn't require a re-render. Paper color changes are different though\u2014they impact the KM computation in the render loop, so they do trigger a full render. If I localize the render function, paper color changes would only update cells in the active rectangle, leaving cells outside with stale colors. I need to implement a \"force full render\" option specifically for paper color changes, so let me search through the codebase to find all render calls.\n\nLooking at the call sites: rebuildScale forces a render after resetting the simulation with fresh image data, which works fine with localized rendering since the active rectangle is empty. The rewet function sets the active rectangle to full canvas before rendering. Only the paper color change at line 2287 actually needs to force a full re-render. So my plan is to add a forceFull parameter to the render function\u2014when false, it only iterates the active rectangle, and when true, it processes the entire canvas. For paper color changes, I'll call render(true) to ensure the new color applies everywhere.\n\nFor the updateBars throttling, I realize the loop itself is the expensive part (O(N) fused pass), not the DOM updates. The totals only get read during idle checks which happen every IDLE_CHECK_INTERVAL frames, so refreshing them at 10Hz is plenty. I'll throttle updateBars to run every 6 frames. I should also add an early-return at the top of simStep when the active rectangle is empty, since the function currently calls 5 other functions that each do their own early-returns anyway. For pressure decay in updateVelocity, I can localize it to only process the painted regions instead of iterating the full array.\n\nNow for the UI improvements: I'm adding a floating toggle button to hide and show the control panel.     "input": {
      "description": "Localize render to active rect, with forceFull escape hatch for paper-color change",
      "old_str": "function render() {\n  const data = imgData.data;\n  const P0 = PIGMENTS[0], P1 = PIGMENTS[1], P2 = PIGMENTS[2];\n\n  // Alpha fade-in for the lowest pigment amounts. Cells with xt above\n  // this threshold render fully opaque (so painted regions look exactly\n  // the way they would over an opaque cream canvas \u2014 KM math unchanged);\n  // cells with xt = 0 go fully transparent (body color shows through);\n  // a thin transition band gives anti-aliased edges instead of a hard cut.\n  const ALPHA_FULL_AT = 0.012;\n  const ALPHA_SCALE = 255 / ALPHA_FULL_AT;\n\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    const x0 = g[0][i] + d[0][i];\n    const x1 = g[1][i] + d[1][i];\n    const x2 = g[2][i] + d[2][i];\n    const xt = x0 + x1 + x2;\n    const ph = paperH[i];\n\n    // Paper texture: subtle warm variation\n    const tex = (ph - 0.5) * 0.06;\n    const PR = PAPER_R_BASE + tex;\n    const PG = PAPER_G_BASE + tex;\n    const PB = PAPER_B_BASE + tex;\n\n    let R, G, B;\n\n    if (xt < 0.004) {\n      // Just paper (with possible dampness sheen)\n      const dampSheen = wet[i] * 0.018;\n      R = PR + dampSheen;\n      G = PG + dampSheen;\n      B = PB + dampSheen * 1.2;\n    } else {\n      const inv = 1 / xt;\n      const w0 = x0 * inv, w1 = x1 * inv, w2 = x2 * inv;\n      // Weighted K and S\n      const Kr = P0.K[0]*w0 + P1.K[0]*w1 + P2.K[0]*w2;\n      const Kg = P0.K[1]*w0 + P1.K[1]*w1 + P2.K[1]*w2;\n      const Kb = P0.K[2]*w0 + P1.K[2]*w1 + P2.K[2]*w2;\n      const Sr = P0.S[0]*w0 + P1.S[0]*w1 + P2.S[0]*w2;\n      const Sg = P0.S[1]*w0 + P1.S[1]*w1 + P2.S[1]*w2;\n      const Sb = P0.S[2]*w0 + P1.S[2]*w1 + P2.S[2]*w2;\n\n      const thickness = xt < 4 ? xt : 4;\n\n      R = kmReflect(Kr, Sr, thickness, PR);\n      G = kmReflect(Kg, Sg, thickness, PG);\n      B = kmReflect(Kb, Sb, thickness, PB);\n    }\n\n    const j = i * 4;\n    data[j]     = R * 255;\n    data[j + 1] = G * 255;\n    data[j + 2] = B * 255;\n    data[j + 3] = xt >= ALPHA_FULL_AT ? 255 : (xt * ALPHA_SCALE);\n  }\n\n  renderCtx.putImageData(imgData, 0, 0);\n  // Clear the display canvas so transparent regions of the new frame\n  // don't blend over stale pixels from the previous frame.\n  ctx.clearRect(0, 0, DISPLAY_W, DISPLAY_H);\n  ctx.drawImage(renderCanvas, 0, 0, DISPLAY_W, DISPLAY_H);\n}",
      "new_str": "// render(forceFull=false) \u2014 write RGBA pixels to imgData, then push to\n// renderCanvas and scale to the display canvas.\n//\n// v0.10 localization: when the active rect is non-empty and forceFull\n// is false, the per-cell KM/alpha loop iterates only over the active\n// rect. Cells outside the rect have frozen state (active rect tracks\n// all dynamics by definition), so the imgData bytes left over from\n// when they WERE rendered still match their current sim state.\n//\n// putImageData transfers the full imgData buffer each call regardless\n// \u2014 its cost is a memcpy, dwarfed by the per-cell KM math we just\n// avoided. The scale to the display canvas is GPU work, also fixed.\n//\n// forceFull is set by callers that change something which affects\n// pixels outside the active rect \u2014 currently just paper color change.\n// The \"Transparent\" toggle only changes body CSS, not canvas pixels.\nfunction render(forceFull) {\n  const data = imgData.data;\n  const P0 = PIGMENTS[0], P1 = PIGMENTS[1], P2 = PIGMENTS[2];\n\n  // Alpha fade-in for the lowest pigment amounts. Cells with xt above\n  // this threshold render fully opaque (so painted regions look exactly\n  // the way they would over an opaque cream canvas \u2014 KM math unchanged);\n  // cells with xt = 0 go fully transparent (body color shows through);\n  // a thin transition band gives anti-aliased edges instead of a hard cut.\n  const ALPHA_FULL_AT = 0.012;\n  const ALPHA_SCALE = 255 / ALPHA_FULL_AT;\n\n  // Determine iteration bounds. Empty rect + !forceFull = skip the per-cell\n  // loop entirely; imgData is already correct for the frozen state, so\n  // pushing it again would just waste a memcpy + draw.\n  let yStart, yEnd, xStart, xEnd, isFull;\n  if (forceFull) {\n    yStart = 0; yEnd = GH - 1;\n    xStart = 0; xEnd = GW - 1;\n    isFull = true;\n  } else if (activeRectIsEmpty()) {\n    return;\n  } else {\n    yStart = Math.max(0, activeMinY);\n    yEnd   = Math.min(GH - 1, activeMaxY);\n    xStart = Math.max(0, activeMinX);\n    xEnd   = Math.min(GW - 1, activeMaxX);\n    isFull = (yStart === 0 && yEnd === GH - 1 && xStart === 0 && xEnd === GW - 1);\n  }\n\n  // Hoist the array refs once. The original loop accessed g[k][i] via\n  // two-level lookup; localized or not, the cached refs help.\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n\n  for (let y = yStart; y <= yEnd; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    for (let x = xStart; x <= xEnd; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      const x0 = g0[i] + d0[i];\n      const x1 = g1[i] + d1[i];\n      const x2 = g2[i] + d2[i];\n      const xt = x0 + x1 + x2;\n      const ph = paperH[i];\n\n      // Paper texture: subtle warm variation\n      const tex = (ph - 0.5) * 0.06;\n      const PR = PAPER_R_BASE + tex;\n      const PG = PAPER_G_BASE + tex;\n      const PB = PAPER_B_BASE + tex;\n\n      let R, G, B;\n\n      if (xt < 0.004) {\n        // Just paper (with possible dampness sheen)\n        const dampSheen = wet[i] * 0.018;\n        R = PR + dampSheen;\n        G = PG + dampSheen;\n        B = PB + dampSheen * 1.2;\n      } else {\n        const inv = 1 / xt;\n        const w0 = x0 * inv, w1 = x1 * inv, w2 = x2 * inv;\n        // Weighted K and S\n        const Kr = P0.K[0]*w0 + P1.K[0]*w1 + P2.K[0]*w2;\n        const Kg = P0.K[1]*w0 + P1.K[1]*w1 + P2.K[1]*w2;\n        const Kb = P0.K[2]*w0 + P1.K[2]*w1 + P2.K[2]*w2;\n        const Sr = P0.S[0]*w0 + P1.S[0]*w1 + P2.S[0]*w2;\n        const Sg = P0.S[1]*w0 + P1.S[1]*w1 + P2.S[1]*w2;\n        const Sb = P0.S[2]*w0 + P1.S[2]*w1 + P2.S[2]*w2;\n\n        const thickness = xt < 4 ? xt : 4;\n\n        R = kmReflect(Kr, Sr, thickness, PR);\n        G = kmReflect(Kg, Sg, thickness, PG);\n        B = kmReflect(Kb, Sb, thickness, PB);\n      }\n\n      const j = i * 4;\n      data[j]     = R * 255;\n      data[j + 1] = G * 255;\n      data[j + 2] = B * 255;\n      data[j + 3] = xt >= ALPHA_FULL_AT ? 255 : (xt * ALPHA_SCALE);\n    }\n  }\n\n  // Push to renderCanvas. dirtyRect form of putImageData transfers only\n  // the active sub-region \u2014 useful when localized, no-op when full.\n  if (isFull) {\n    renderCtx.putImageData(imgData, 0, 0);\n  } else {\n    const dw = xEnd - xStart + 1;\n    const dh = yEnd - yStart + 1;\n    renderCtx.putImageData(imgData, 0, 0, xStart, yStart, dw, dh);\n  }\n  // Clear the display canvas so transparent regions of the new frame\n  // don't blend over stale pixels from the previous frame.\n  ctx.clearRect(0, 0, DISPLAY_W, DISPLAY_H);\n  ctx.drawImage(renderCanvas, 0, 0, DISPLAY_W, DISPLAY_H);\n}",

Previously this was two separate\n  2392\t  // O(N) loops (updateDrynessBar + updatePigmentBar); merging them\n  2393\t  // halves the bar-update cost and keeps wet[i] / g[k][i] / d[k][i]\n  2394\t  // each touched once per frame instead of twice.\n  2395\t  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  2396\t  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n  2397\t  let tWet = 0, tSusp = 0, tPig = 0;\n  2398\t  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n  2399\t    tWet += wet[i];\n  2400\t    const s = g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\n  2401\t    tSusp += s;\n  2402\t    tPig += s + d0[i] + d1[i] + d2[i];\n  2403\t  }\n  2404\t  lastTotalWet = tWet;\n  2405\t  lastTotalSuspended = tSusp;\n  2406\t\n  2407\t  // --- Dryness ---\n  2408\t  const avgWet = tWet / N;\n  2409\t  let dry = 1 - avgWet / DRYNESS_REFERENCE;\n  2410\t  if (dry < 0) dry = 0; else if (dry > 1) dry = 1;\n  2411\t  drynessFill.style.width = (dry * 100).toFixed(1) + '%';\n  2412\t\n  2413\t  // --- Pigment ---\n  2414\t  const avgPig = tPig / N;\n  2415\t  // Expose to the AI planner so it can switch behavior on saturation\n  2416\t  currentAvgPigment = avgPig;\n  2417\t  let frac = avgPig / PIGMENT_REFERENCE;\n  2418\t  if (frac < 0) frac = 0; else if (frac > 1) frac = 1;\n  2419\t  pigmentFill.style.width = (frac * 100).toFixed(1) + '%';\n  2420\t  // Readout shows raw average pigment per cell. Previously this was two separate\\n  // O(N) loops (updateDrynessBar + updatePigmentBar); merging them\\n  // halves the bar-update cost and keeps wet[i] / g[k][i] / d[k][i]\\n  // each touched once per frame instead of twice.\\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\\n  let tWet = 0, tSusp = 0, tPig = 0;\\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\\n    tWet += wet[i];\\n    const s = g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\\n    tSusp += s;\\n    tPig += s + d0[i] + d1[i] + d2[i];\\n  }\\n  lastTotalWet = tWet;\\n  lastTotalSuspended = tSusp;\\n\\n  // --- Dryness ---\\n  const avgWet = tWet / N;\\n  let dry = 1 - avgWet / DRYNESS_REFERENCE;\\n  if (dry < 0) dry = 0; else if (dry > 1) dry = 1;\\n  drynessFill.style.width = (dry * 100).toFixed(1) + '%';\\n\\n  // --- Pigment ---\\n  const avgPig = tPig / N;\\n  // Expose to the AI planner so it can switch behavior on saturation\\n  currentAvgPigment = avgPig;\\n  let frac = avgPig / PIGMENT_REFERENCE;\\n  if (frac < 0) frac = 0; else if (frac > 1) frac = 1;\\n  pigmentFill.style.width = (frac * 100).toFixed(1) + '%';\\n  // Readout shows raw average pigment per cell. Theoretical max is 3.0\\n  // (all three pigments saturated everywhere); in practice values\\n  // above ~1.0 indicate very heavy coverage.\\n  pigmentReadout.textContent = avgPig.toFixed(3);\\n}\\n\\n// ============================================================\\n// IDLE SKIP \\u2014 sim/render skipping when canvas is settled\\n// ============================================================\\n// Most of a typical session, nothing is happening: the canvas is dry,\\n// no pigment is moving, no input is arriving. Running simStep + render\\n// every frame in that state burns CPU for no visible change.       "description": "Localize pressure decay to active rect; expand bounds to be safe for edge darkening reads",
      "new_str": "function updateVelocity() {\n  if (activeRectIsEmpty()) {\n    // No active dynamics anywhere: pressure is already \u2248 0 outside the\n    // (empty) rect by definition. Skip entirely.\n    return;\n  }\n  const y0 = Math.max(1, activeMinY);\n  const y1 = Math.min(GH - 2, activeMaxY);\n  const x0 = Math.max(1, activeMinX);\n  const x1 = Math.min(GW - 2, activeMaxX);\n  for (let y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    for (let x = x0; x <= x1; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      if (wet[i] < 0.04) { u_new[i] = 0; v_new[i] = 0; continue; }\n      // pressure gradient\n      const dpdx = pressure[i + 1] - pressure[i - 1];\n      const dpdy = pressure[i + GW] - pressure[i - GW];\n      // paper slope (gradient of height field)\n      const dhdx = paperH[i + 1] - paperH[i - 1];\n      const dhdy = paperH[i + GW] - paperH[i - GW];\n      // viscous diffusion (laplacian)\n      const lapU = u[i - 1] + u[i + 1] + u[i - GW] + u[i + GW] - 4 * u[i];\n      const lapV = v[i - 1] + v[i + 1] + v[i - GW] + v[i + GW] - 4 * v[i];\n\n      let nu = u[i] + DT * (-dpdx * 0.5 - dhdx * PAPER_TILT + VISCOSITY * lapU - DRAG * u[i]);\n      let nv = v[i] + DT * (-dpdy * 0.5 - dhdy * PAPER_TILT + VISCOSITY * lapV - DRAG * v[i]);\n      if (nu >  VEL_CLAMP) nu =  VEL_CLAMP; else if (nu < -VEL_CLAMP) nu = -VEL_CLAMP;\n      if (nv >  VEL_CLAMP) nv =  VEL_CLAMP; else if (nv < -VEL_CLAMP) nv = -VEL_CLAMP;\n      u_new[i] = nu; v_new[i] = nv;\n    }\n  }\n  // Copy u_new/v_new back to u/v, restricted to the rect. Float32Array's\n  // set(subarray, offset) is a fast memcpy under the hood. Cells outside\n  // the rect retain their previous u/v values (which are \u2248 0 since the\n  // shrink scan tracks pressure too).\n  for (let y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    const start = yo + x0;\n    const end = yo + x1 + 1;\n    u.set(u_new.subarray(start, end), start);\n    v.set(v_new.subarray(start, end), start);\n  }\n  // v0.10 \u2014 pressure decay restricted to active rect. Pressure is only\n  // injected by paintAt (which always grows the rect to cover the stamp)\n  // and only consumed by updateVelocity + applyEdgeDarkening (both rect-\n  // restricted). Cells outside the rect have pressure \u2248 0 from initial\n  // state; multiplying them by 0.94 was burning N writes per simStep for\n  // no behavioral effect.\n  for (let y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    for (let x = x0; x <= x1; x++) pressure[yo + x] *= 0.94;\n  }\n}",
      "old_str": "function updateVelocity() {\n  if (activeRectIsEmpty()) {\n    // Pressure decay still runs even when no painting \u2014 cells outside\n    // active rect have pressure \u2248 0 anyway, so this is mostly a no-op.\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) pressure[i] *= 0.94;\n    return;\n  }\n  const y0 = Math.max(1, activeMinY);\n  const y1 = Math.min(GH - 2, activeMaxY);\n  const x0 = Math.max(1, activeMinX);\n  const x1 = Math.min(GW - 2, activeMaxX);\n  for (let y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    for (let x = x0; x <= x1; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      if (wet[i] < 0.04) { u_new[i] = 0; v_new[i] = 0; continue; }\n      // pressure gradient\n      const dpdx = pressure[i + 1] - pressure[i - 1];\n      const dpdy = pressure[i + GW] - pressure[i - GW];\n      // paper slope (gradient of height field)\n      const dhdx = paperH[i + 1] - paperH[i - 1];\n      const dhdy = paperH[i + GW] - paperH[i - GW];\n      // viscous diffusion (laplacian)\n      const lapU = u[i - 1] + u[i + 1] + u[i - GW] + u[i + GW] - 4 * u[i];\n      const lapV = v[i - 1] + v[i + 1] + v[i - GW] + v[i + GW] - 4 * v[i];\n\n      let nu = u[i] + DT * (-dpdx * 0.5 - dhdx * PAPER_TILT + VISCOSITY * lapU - DRAG * u[i]);\n      let nv = v[i] + DT * (-dpdy * 0.5 - dhdy * PAPER_TILT + VISCOSITY * lapV - DRAG * v[i]);\n      if (nu >  VEL_CLAMP) nu =  VEL_CLAMP; else if (nu < -VEL_CLAMP) nu = -VEL_CLAMP;\n      if (nv >  VEL_CLAMP) nv =  VEL_CLAMP; else if (nv < -VEL_CLAMP) nv = -VEL_CLAMP;\n      u_new[i] = nu; v_new[i] = nv;\n    }\n  }\n  // Copy u_new/v_new back to u/v, restricted to the rect. Float32Array's\n  // set(subarray, offset) is a fast memcpy under the hood. Cells outside\n  // the rect retain their previous u/v values (which are \u2248 0 since the\n  // shrink scan tracks pressure too).\n  for (let y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    const start = yo + x0;\n    const end = yo + x1 + 1;\n    u.set(u_new.subarray(start, end), start);\n    v.set(v_new.subarray(start, end), start);\n  }\n  // pressure decays toward zero\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) pressure[i] *= 0.94;\n}",

    "thinking": " I'm weighing whether to limit the evaporate function to just the active rectangle instead of processing the entire canvas. If I do that, painted regions will still dry out properly since they're within the active area, but unpainted regions will remain stuck at their initial wetness level\u2014which means the dryness bar would plateau somewhere around 60-80% instead of reaching fully dry.\n\nThe real question is what the dryness bar should represent. Right now it's averaging across the whole canvas, so with localized evaporation, it would never hit 100% since unpainted areas stay wet. That's a meaningful behavior change, though users do have the Dry button for instant full drying if they need it.\n\nI could split the difference: keep evaporating the full canvas with a simple decay operation, but reserve the more expensive pigment-handling path for just the active rectangle. That way I get the CPU savings without changing how the canvas dries visually.\n\nBut that means two separate loops, which might actually be slower than the current single pass. The existing loop is already pretty lean\u2014most cells just do a multiply and write, and the pigment dump branch almost never triggers anyway. Localizing to the active rect would skip a lot of those cheap operations, saving maybe 2 million operations per frame at typical canvas sizes, but I need to weigh that against the added complexity.\n\nThe real issue is the dryness bar calculation. If I only decay painted regions, unpainted wet cells still contribute to the average, which throws off the user's perception of dryness. The math gets messy\u2014painted areas might be completely dry while the overall average stays inflated by all that untouched wet sitting around.\n\nI could either stick with global evaporation (which dries everything uniformly) or bite the bullet and recalculate the dryness metric to only consider painted pixels. The two-loop approach might be worth it to avoid the UX regression where the dryness bar caps out lower than before.\n\nActually, let me just keep evaporate running across the full canvas but make sure it's implemented efficiently. Let me look at the current implementation...\n\nChecking the evaporate function...\n\nThe inner loop that handles the dry-out path is a bit inelegant with that unrolled pigment transfer, but since that branch rarely executes, the hot path is just the decay calculation which is already quite tight. I'll leave it as is.\n\nFor fadeStep, I should localize the pigment array since values outside the rectangle are zero by definition anyway\u2014the shrink and expand operations maintain that invariant.\n\nActually wait, I'm realizing a potential issue. Shrink only runs every 30 frames and trims based on the gradient threshold or pressure, but it doesn't check the pigment values themselves. So if a user paints, the rectangle grows, pigment settles from the gradient into the pigment array, then the gradient drops below threshold and shrink runs, the pigment array could still have nonzero values outside the rectangle bounds. That's a bug I need to trace through.\n\nOnce cells fall outside the active rectangle, they stop getting updated by the transfer and movement operations, but evaporation still runs on them. The render reads both gradient and pigment values, but if those cells are outside the rectangle, the pixels never get drawn anyway. So I'm stuck with orphaned pigment data that's invisible but still taking up memory.\n\nThe real issue is with the fade step\u2014if I localize it to just the rectangle, pigment outside it won't fade, leaving ghost traces on the canvas. I need to either fade everything globally or expand the shrink scan to consider pigment concentration when deciding what counts as \"active,\" not just gradient and pressure. I'm weighing the tradeoffs between keeping fadeStep running at full resolution versus trying to optimize the active rectangle. Option A seems cleanest\u2014running fadeStep at 10Hz across the full grid is cheap enough, and it avoids the complexity of either keeping dried pigment cells in the active rect forever or missing fade updates on cells outside it.\n\nActually, let me reconsider: I could gate the shrink scan to include dried pigment only when fade is enabled, and localize fadeStep accordingly. That way when fade is off, dried pigment stays frozen outside the rect and fadeStep doesn't run at all, but when fade is on, those cells stay active and get properly updated. This feels like the right balance. ves real cycles. Let me implement that approach instead.     "text": "**v0.10 changes:**\n\n**Defaults**\n- `SCALE = 1.75` is now the slider default (also via `?scale=` URL param).\n\n**Perf**\n- **Localized `render`** \u2014 the per-cell KM/alpha loop now iterates the active rect, not the full grid. Cells outside the rect have frozen state (the rect tracks all dynamics by definition), so their stale `imgData` bytes still match the truth. `putImageData` is called with a dirty-rect to transfer only the changed sub-region. In the benchmark above, a stroke covering ~1% of the canvas dropped render from ~22 ms to ~0.75 ms per call (29\u00d7 faster). Paper color change calls `render(true)` to force a full re-paint since `PAPER_*_BASE` affects every cell. `resetSim` now also clears `imgData.data` + the display canvas so stale pixels from before the reset don't leak through.\n- **Bars throttled to ~10 Hz** (`BARS_TICK_INTERVAL = 6`). The fused O(N) sum was running every frame just to update CSS widths; the bars' 0.25 s ease-out transitions smooth the sparser updates, and `shouldRunSim`'s idle check only consults `lastTotalWet`/`lastTotalSuspended` every 30 frames, so 10 Hz is more than fresh.\n- **Pressure decay restricted to active rect.** Pressure is only ever injected by `paintAt` (which grows the rect) and consumed by `updateVelocity` + `applyEdgeDarkening` (both rect-restricted). Multiplying the rest of the grid by 0.94 was burning N writes per `simStep` for no visible change.\n- **`simStep` top-level early-return** when rect is empty. Each sub-step already early-returns, but skipping the 5 function dispatches saves call overhead. `evaporate` still runs so global drying continues.\n\n**UI**\n- **Hide-controls button** in the top-right (the `\u2212`/`+` glyph). Click it or press **H** to toggle every chrome element \u2014 main panel, dryness bar, pigment bar, readout \u2014 with an 0.18 s fade. The button itself stays clickable so you can always bring the UI back. Form fields (color picker, sliders) intercept `H` so it doesn't fire while you're editing them.\n\n**Not localized (deliberate):**\n- `evaporate` \u2014 would leave unpainted areas wet forever, breaking the \"dryness eventually reaches 100%\" behavior of the bar.\n- `fadeStep` \u2014 only runs at 10 Hz when Fade is on; deposited pigment can sit outside the active rect (the shrink scan only tracks `g` and `pressure`), so localizing would create ghost pigment that doesn't fade.\n- `applyEdgeDarkening`'s box-blur passes \u2014 the running-sum algorithm needs full rows/columns; the apply step on top is already rect-restricted.",

      "description": "Add Export PNG handler and exportPng function",
      "new_str": "// Dry button \u2014 inverse of Re-wet. Same momentary-flash treatment.\nconst dryBtn = document.getElementById('dry-btn');\ndryBtn.addEventListener('click', () => {\n  dryPaper();\n  dryBtn.classList.add('on');\n  setTimeout(() => dryBtn.classList.remove('on'), 220);\n});\n\n// ============================================================\n// PNG EXPORT (v0.10.1)\n// ============================================================\n// Snapshots the current canvas to a downloadable PNG file. Two\n// subtleties make this more than a one-liner:\n//\n//   1. The localized render only writes pixels for cells in the active\n//      rect \u2014 cells that have never been painted have zero alpha in\n//      imgData / renderCanvas. We force a full render first so blank\n//      paper picks up its texture and dampness sheen and exports as\n//      proper opaque paper, not transparent holes.\n//\n//   2. When the Transparent toggle is off, the user sees the painting\n//      sitting on the body-bg paper color. The display canvas itself\n//      has alpha holes wherever pigment is sparse; the paper color\n//      shines through via CSS, not via the canvas pixels. For an\n//      export that looks like what the user sees, we composite the\n//      paper color underneath before encoding. Transparent toggle on:\n//      preserve alpha so the export can be layered elsewhere.\nfunction exportPng() {\n  // Force a full render \u2014 bypass the active-rect optimization so every\n  // cell has fresh, accurate pixels. This temporarily costs one full-\n  // grid render pass (a few ms at 1.5M cells); cheap for an export.\n  render(true);\n\n  // Offscreen canvas at display dimensions. Using display rather than\n  // grid resolution gives the export the same size the user sees on\n  // screen; the upscale uses the same imageSmoothing the live canvas\n  // does, so the look matches.\n  const out = document.createElement('canvas');\n  out.width  = DISPLAY_W;\n  out.height = DISPLAY_H;\n  const octx = out.getContext('2d');\n  octx.imageSmoothingEnabled = true;\n  octx.imageSmoothingQuality = 'high';\n\n  if (!transparentBg) {\n    // Paint paper color underneath so the export is self-contained.\n    // Uses the same hex form the body-bg CSS variable uses, so the\n    // background tone matches the on-screen rendering exactly.\n    octx.fillStyle = rgbToHex(PAPER_R_BASE, PAPER_G_BASE, PAPER_B_BASE);\n    octx.fillRect(0, 0, DISPLAY_W, DISPLAY_H);\n  }\n\n  // Draw the freshly-rendered low-res canvas, scaled to display size.\n  // Same drawImage path the live preview uses inside render().\n  octx.drawImage(renderCanvas, 0, 0, DISPLAY_W, DISPLAY_H);\n\n  // Encode to PNG and trigger a download via a temporary <a>. The\n  // timestamp in the filename keeps successive saves from clobbering\n  // each other in the user's download folder.\n  out.toBlob((blob) => {\n    if (!blob) return;\n    const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);\n    const a = document.createElement('a');\n    const stamp = new Date().toISOString().replace(/[:.]/g, '-').slice(0, 19);\n    a.href = url;\n    a.download = `watercolor-${stamp}.png`;\n    document.body.appendChild(a);\n    a.click();\n    document.body.removeChild(a);\n    // Defer revoke briefly so the browser has time to start the\n    // download stream \u2014 immediate revoke can race on some engines.\n    setTimeout(() => URL.revokeObjectURL(url), 1000);\n  }, 'image/png');\n}\n\nconst exportBtn = document.getElementById('export-btn');\nexportBtn.addEventListener('click', () => {\n  exportPng();\n  // Brief flash for visual confirmation \u2014 same convention as Re-wet/Dry.\n  exportBtn.classList.add('on');\n  setTimeout(() => exportBtn.classList.remove('on'), 220);\n});",

    "text": "**v0.10.1 \u2014 PNG export**\n\nAdded an **Export PNG** button to the bottom controls row, next to Re-wet / Dry / Reset. Two subtleties handled:\n\n1. **Forces a full render first.** The localized render only writes pixels for cells in the active rect, so blank paper has alpha=0 in `imgData`. Without forcing a full pass, never-touched areas would export as transparent holes instead of textured paper. The cost is one full-grid render pass (a few ms) on the export click \u2014 cheap.\n\n2. **Composites the paper color underneath when Transparent is off.** What you see on screen is the painting drawn over body-bg (paper color via CSS), not painting drawn over opaque paper pixels \u2014 the canvas itself has alpha holes wherever pigment is sparse. So on export, if Transparent is off, the export canvas is filled with `PAPER_*_BASE` first, then the render canvas is composited on top. The PNG matches what's on screen. If Transparent is on, alpha is preserved so the export can be layered elsewhere.\n\nFiles download as `watercolor-YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SS.png` so successive saves don't clobber each other. The download uses a temporary `<a>` + `URL.createObjectURL` with a deferred revoke to avoid racing the browser's download stream.",

The\n  2339\t// path is a quadratic Bezier with a perpendicular control-point offset\n  2340\t// so strokes have some natural curve. The AI calls paintAt directly \u2014\n  2341\t// same code path the user's pointer takes \u2014 so it can't desync from\n  2342\t// or bypass the simulation in any way.\n  2343\t\n  2344\tlet aiEnabled = false;\n  2345\tlet aiState = null;\n  2346\t// Average pigment per cell \u2014 updated each frame by updateBars.\n  2347\t// Shared with the AI planner so it can switch behavior based on canvas\n  2348\t// saturation without re-computing the sum.\n  2349\tlet currentAvgPigment = 0;\n  2350\t// Above this saturation, the AI stops adding pigment and exclusively\n  2351\t// uses the water brush ('soften' strategy) \u2014 pushing existing paint\n  2352\t// around rather than piling more on a saturated canvas.\n  2353\tconst AI_WATER_THRESHOLD = 0.75;\n  2354\t\n  2355\tconst AI_STRATEGIES = [\n  2356\t  // brushPx is display pixels (clamped to MAX_BRUSH at plan time).\n  2357\t  // dist is a fraction of min(GW, GH) \u2192 stroke length in grid cells.\n  2358\t  // dur is 22], dist: [0.00, 0.05], dur: [ 8, 20], strength: 0.40, pigment: null         },\n  2362\t  { name: 'soften', weight: 1.0, brushPx: [ 40, 100], dist: [0.05, 0.25], dur: [25, 45], strength: 0.30, pigment: WATER_INDEX  },\n  2363\t  { name: 'lift',   weight: 0.6, brushPx: [ 30,  70], dist: [0.00, 0.10], dur: [15, 30], strength: 0.30, pigment: LIFT_INDEX   },\n  2364\t];\n  2365\t// Pre-cached The\\n// path is a quadratic Bezier with a perpendicular control-point offset\\n// so strokes have some natural curve. The AI calls paintAt directly \\u2014\\n// same code path the user's pointer takes \\u2014 so it can't desync from\\n// or bypass the simulation in any way.\\n\\nlet aiEnabled = false;\\nlet aiState = fraction of min(GW, GH) \\u2192 stroke length in grid cells.\\n  // dur is frame count for the whole stroke. pigment: null = random pigment.\\n  { name: 'mark',   weight: 3.0, brushPx: [ 20,  60],     },\\n  { name: 'detail', weight: 1.6, brushPx: [  8,  22], dist: [0.00, 0.05], dur: [ 8, 20], strength: 0.40, pigment: null         },\\n  { name: 'soften', weight: 1.0, brushPx: [ 40, 100], dist: },\\n];\\n// Pre-cached soften strategy so we don't .find() it on every stroke\\n// once the canvas is saturated.\\nconst AI_SOFTEN_STRATEGY = AI_STRATEGIES.find(s => s.name ===     "id": "toolu_01Y7rY2G5BziBGhjmY66UNAd",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Replace AI toggle wiring with animation dropdown + rainy preset + dispatcher",
      "new_str": "// ----- RAINY preset -----------------------------------------------------\n// A pool of independent \"drops\" that spawn just above the canvas, fall\n// downward with a slight horizontal drift, and paint along the way. The\n// preset uses paintAt the same way the user's pointer does, so every\n// drop participates in the watercolor sim \u2014 wet bleed, edge darkening,\n// granulation all apply per drop.\n//\n// Tuning notes:\n//   \u2022 RAIN_MAX_DROPS caps concurrency so the active rect stays bounded\n//     and the canvas doesn't saturate instantly. 12 feels like steady\n//     rain without obliterating the surface.\n//   \u2022 Per-frame spawn probability is RAIN_SPAWN_PER_SEC / 60, capped by\n//     the max. The math is loose \u2014 a Bernoulli trial per frame, not a\n//     true Poisson \u2014 which is fine for visual density.\n//   \u2022 Palette is cool: cerulean dominates, water-brush drops produce\n//     lift highlights, occasional rose breaks the monotony. The cool\n//     bias reads as a rainy mood whatever paper color is selected.\n//   \u2022 Drops drift slightly horizontally (vx) and fall fast (vy), with\n//     randomness so they don't all march in lockstep.\n\nconst RAIN_MAX_DROPS = 12;\nconst RAIN_SPAWN_PER_SEC = 30;   // average new drops per second (capped by MAX)\n\n// Each entry: [pigmentConstant, weight, [strengthLo, strengthHi], [brushPxLo, brushPxHi]]\nconst RAIN_DROP_TYPES = [\n  // pigmentConstant uses the same indexing paintAt accepts:\n  // 0/1/2 are real pigments; WATER_INDEX is the water brush.\n  // The cerulean index (2) and rose index (0) are hard-coded \u2014 they map to the\n  // PIGMENTS array defined at the top of the script.\n  [2,            0.60, [0.18, 0.30], [10, 22]],   // cerulean \u2014 dominant\n  [WATER_INDEX,  0.30, [0.15, 0.28], [10, 20]],   // water \u2014 lifts existing paint\n  [0,            0.10, [0.10, 0.20], [ 8, 14]],   // rose \u2014 occasional warmth\n];\n\nlet rainDrops = [];\n\nfunction rainSpawnDrop() {\n  // Weighted pick. Sum once and walk; cheap enough at 3 entries.\n  let total = 0;\n  for (const t of RAIN_DROP_TYPES) total += t[1];\n  let r = Math.random() * total;\n  let chosen = RAIN_DROP_TYPES[0];\n  for (const t of RAIN_DROP_TYPES) {\n    r -= t[1];\n    if (r <= 0) { chosen = t; break; }\n  }\n  const [pigment, , [sLo, sHi], [bLo, bHi]] = chosen;\n  return {\n    // Start above the top edge so the drop fades in via the falling\n    // motion rather than popping into existence at y=0.\n    x: Math.random() * GW,\n    y: -10 - Math.random() * 20,\n    vx: (Math.random() - 0.5) * 0.6,            // slight horizontal drift\n    vy: 4.0 + Math.random() * 5.0,              // grid cells per frame\n    brushPx: bLo + Math.random() * (bHi - bLo),\n    pigment,\n    strength: sLo + Math.random() * (sHi - sLo),\n  };\n}\n\nfunction rainStep() {\n  // Spawn up to MAX with per-frame Bernoulli probability. The while-loop\n  // can spawn multiple per frame if probability is high; with\n  // RAIN_SPAWN_PER_SEC=30 at 60fps it averages 0.5 spawn-attempts/frame.\n  const spawnProb = RAIN_SPAWN_PER_SEC / 60;\n  while (rainDrops.length < RAIN_MAX_DROPS && Math.random() < spawnProb) {\n    rainDrops.push(rainSpawnDrop());\n  }\n\n  // Cache the px\u2192grid conversion once per frame. paintAt uses grid\n  // coordinates; the slider/UI works in display pixels.\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n\n  // Step each drop. Walk backward so splice doesn't disturb indices.\n  for (let i = rainDrops.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {\n    const d = rainDrops[i];\n    d.x += d.vx;\n    d.y += d.vy;\n\n    // Cull drops that have run off the canvas (mostly through the\n    // bottom; sideways too, in case the drift carried them off).\n    if (d.y > GH + 10 || d.x < -10 || d.x > GW + 10) {\n      rainDrops.splice(i, 1);\n      continue;\n    }\n\n    // Paint only when the drop is actually on-canvas. The above-canvas\n    // segment is a \"spawn delay\" so drops appear to fall in from off-screen.\n    if (d.y >= 0 && d.y < GH) {\n      paintAt(d.x, d.y, d.brushPx * pxToGridRadius, d.pigment, d.strength);\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// ----- dispatcher -------------------------------------------------------\nfunction animationStep() {\n  switch (animationMode) {\n    case 'ai':    aiStep();   break;\n    case 'rainy': rainStep(); break;\n    // 'off' falls through to no-op\n  }\n}\n\n// ----- UI: animation mode dropdown --------------------------------------\nconst animationSelect = document.getElementById('animation-mode');\nanimationSelect.addEventListener('change', () => {\n  // Discard in-flight preset state so a mode change doesn't continue\n  // a half-finished AI stroke or drop pool from the previous mode.\n  aiState = null;\n  rainDrops = [];\n  animationMode = animationSelect.value;\n});",
      "old_str": "const aiToggle = document.getElementById('toggle-ai');\naiToggle.addEventListener('click', () => {\n  aiEnabled = !aiEnabled;\n  aiToggle.classList.toggle('on', aiEnabled);\n  aiToggle.setAttribute('aria-pressed', String(aiEnabled));\n  if (!aiEnabled) aiState = null;  // discard any in-progress plan\n});",

    "text": "**v0.11 \u2014 Animation Presets dropdown + Rainy**\n\n**UI**\n- New **Animation** dropdown on its own row in the controls panel. Options: `Off`, `AI painter`, `Rainy`. Replaces the old AI toggle button (one mutually-exclusive switch is cleaner than a toggle plus future weather toggles).\n- Styled with a custom SVG chevron and JetBrains Mono caps to match the rest of the panel chrome across platforms.\n\n**Refactor**\n- `aiEnabled` (boolean) \u2192 `animationMode` (string). The AI painter is now just one of several presets, dispatched from a small `animationStep()` switch that runs every frame. Adding new presets means defining a state variable, a `*Step` function, and one `case` arm \u2014 no other plumbing changes.\n- Mode change clears in-flight state (`aiState = null`, `rainDrops = []`) so switching presets mid-stroke doesn't leave half-finished work bleeding into the next mode.\n\n**Rainy preset**\n- Pool of up to **12** concurrent drops. Each drop is an independent painting agent \u2014 its own position, velocity, brush size, pigment, and strength. They share the user-facing `paintAt` path so every drop participates in the full sim (wet bleed, edge darkening, granulation).\n- Drops spawn just above the top edge (`y = -10 to -30`) with a random `x` across the canvas width and fall in at `4\u20139` grid cells/frame, with slight horizontal drift (`vx = \u00b10.3`). The above-canvas segment acts as a natural spawn delay \u2014 drops *fall into* the canvas instead of popping in at `y = 0`.\n- Spawn rate ~30 drops/sec on average via Bernoulli per-frame; gated by the 12-drop cap.\n- **Palette** weighted cool: 60% cerulean (cool blue dominates), 30% water brush (lift highlights \u2014 creates lighter areas where drops hit existing paint, like rain on a wet wash), 10% rose (warm accent for visual variety, mostly used for accents in puddled areas).\n- Drops are culled when they leave the canvas on any side; pool refills from the spawn loop next frame.\n\n**Architecture note for future presets**\n\nThe pattern is: each preset is a self-contained state block + step function. The dispatcher is one switch. So **Sunny** might be slow, warm horizontal sweeps with yellow/orange; **Snowy** could be small slow drops with high water content and rose-tinted whites; **Foggy** might be very large slow soft strokes with the water brush across the bottom third. Each is ~50 lines of code following the same shape as `rainStep`.",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T06:26:39.774715Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T06:27:29.018364Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_01ES1K6CFhbMHqaGxfwfmscg",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Insert all new preset implementations + extended dispatcher + state-clearing on mode change",
      "new_str": "// ----- SUNNY preset -----------------------------------------------------\n// Calm, slow horizontal sweeps in the upper portion of the canvas. Reads\n// as soft sunlight rather than literal sunbeams \u2014 large brush, warm\n// palette (yellow primary, rose accent), low strength so accumulated\n// strokes wash rather than stack into a solid mark. Spawn interval is\n// deliberately long; sunny is the quietest preset.\nconst SUNNY_MAX = 2;\nconst SUNNY_SPAWN_INTERVAL_MIN = 150;\nconst SUNNY_SPAWN_INTERVAL_RAND = 120;\n\nlet sunnyStrokes = [];\nlet sunnySpawnTimer = 30;  // small initial delay so the first stroke isn't instant\n\nfunction sunnySpawn() {\n  // Bezier sweep across canvas, biased toward the top third. Direction\n  // alternates randomly so strokes don't all go the same way.\n  const direction = Math.random() < 0.5 ? 1 : -1;\n  const sx = direction > 0 ? -GW * 0.1 : GW * 1.1;\n  const ex = direction > 0 ? GW * 1.1 : -GW * 0.1;\n  const sy = GH * (0.05 + Math.random() * 0.35);\n  const ey = sy + (Math.random() - 0.5) * GH * 0.15;\n  const cx = (sx + ex) / 2;\n  const cy = (sy + ey) / 2 + (Math.random() - 0.5) * GH * 0.25;\n  return {\n    progress: 0,\n    duration: 80 + Math.floor(Math.random() * 80),\n    sx, sy, cx, cy, ex, ey,\n    pigment: Math.random() < 0.75 ? 1 : 0,        // 75% yellow, 25% rose\n    strength: 0.16 + Math.random() * 0.10,\n    brushPx: 80 + Math.random() * 100,\n  };\n}\n\nfunction sunnyStep() {\n  sunnySpawnTimer--;\n  if (sunnySpawnTimer <= 0 && sunnyStrokes.length < SUNNY_MAX) {\n    sunnyStrokes.push(sunnySpawn());\n    sunnySpawnTimer = SUNNY_SPAWN_INTERVAL_MIN + Math.floor(Math.random() * SUNNY_SPAWN_INTERVAL_RAND);\n  }\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  for (let i = sunnyStrokes.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {\n    const s = sunnyStrokes[i];\n    s.progress++;\n    if (s.progress > s.duration) { sunnyStrokes.splice(i, 1); continue; }\n    const t = s.progress / s.duration;\n    const u = 1 - t;\n    const x = u*u*s.sx + 2*u*t*s.cx + t*t*s.ex;\n    const y = u*u*s.sy + 2*u*t*s.cy + t*t*s.ey;\n    paintAt(x, y, s.brushPx * pxToGridRadius, s.pigment, s.strength);\n  }\n}\n\n// ----- WINDY preset -----------------------------------------------------\n// Many fast horizontal streaks. The wind has a dominant direction that\n// occasionally gusts the other way \u2014 a small probability per frame of\n// flipping windDirection produces irregular shifts rather than the\n// strict left/right alternation you'd get with a sine wave.\nconst WINDY_MAX = 8;\nconst WINDY_GUST_PROB = 0.0008;\n\nlet windyStreaks = [];\nlet windDirection = 1;\n\nfunction windySpawn() {\n  // Spawn just off the upwind edge so streaks \"blow in\"\n  return {\n    x: windDirection > 0 ? -10 - Math.random() * 30 : GW + 10 + Math.random() * 30,\n    y: Math.random() * GH,\n    vx: windDirection * (5 + Math.random() * 6),\n    vy: (Math.random() - 0.5) * 0.3,\n    brushPx: 6 + Math.random() * 14,\n    // Cool-air cerulean dominates, occasional warm yellow streak. Water\n    // adds variation by lifting deposited paint (visible only where the\n    // canvas already has pigment, but that's the point \u2014 wind erodes).\n    pigment: (() => {\n      const r = Math.random();\n      if (r < 0.70) return 2;            // cerulean\n      if (r < 0.85) return WATER_INDEX;  // water lift\n      return 1;                          // yellow\n    })(),\n    strength: 0.10 + Math.random() * 0.10,\n    life: 200,\n  };\n}\n\nfunction windyStep() {\n  if (Math.random() < WINDY_GUST_PROB) windDirection *= -1;\n  while (windyStreaks.length < WINDY_MAX && Math.random() < 0.35) {\n    windyStreaks.push(windySpawn());\n  }\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  for (let i = windyStreaks.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {\n    const s = windyStreaks[i];\n    s.x += s.vx;\n    s.y += s.vy;\n    s.life--;\n    if (s.life <= 0 || s.x < -20 || s.x > GW + 20) { windyStreaks.splice(i, 1); continue; }\n    if (s.y >= 0 && s.y < GH) {\n      paintAt(s.x, s.y, s.brushPx * pxToGridRadius, s.pigment, s.strength);\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// ----- THUNDERSTORM preset ---------------------------------------------\n// Heavy rain (faster, more drops than the rainy preset) plus periodic\n// lightning. Lightning is a single-frame burst of small paintAt calls\n// along a jagged vertical path \u2014 not a separate visual primitive,\n// just dense small strokes in bright yellow that read as a bolt\n// against the dark/wet canvas.\nconst STORM_MAX_DROPS = 18;\nconst STORM_SPAWN_PER_SEC = 60;\nconst STORM_LIGHTNING_MIN_COOLDOWN = 90;     // frames; ~1.5 sec min between flashes\nconst STORM_LIGHTNING_RAND_COOLDOWN = 360;   // additional random delay\n\nlet stormDrops = [];\nlet stormLightningCooldown = 120;            // initial delay before first flash\n\nfunction stormSpawnDrop() {\n  const r = Math.random();\n  let pigment;\n  if (r < 0.65) pigment = 2;              // cerulean \u2014 dominant\n  else if (r < 0.85) pigment = WATER_INDEX; // water lift\n  else pigment = 0;                       // rose\n  return {\n    x: Math.random() * GW,\n    y: -10 - Math.random() * 30,\n    vx: (Math.random() - 0.5) * 1.0,    // more turbulent than rainy\n    vy: 6 + Math.random() * 6,          // harder rain\n    brushPx: 10 + Math.random() * 18,\n    pigment,\n    strength: 0.20 + Math.random() * 0.20,\n  };\n}\n\nfunction stormFlashLightning() {\n  // Build a jagged vertical path top \u2192 bottom; paint many small dabs\n  // along each segment so the bolt reads as a continuous stroke. Yellow\n  // at high strength against the wet, cool canvas pops cleanly.\n  const segments = 6 + Math.floor(Math.random() * 5);\n  const startX = GW * 0.15 + Math.random() * GW * 0.7;\n  const jitter = GW * 0.04;\n  let cx = startX;\n  let cy = -2;\n  const segDY = GH / segments;\n  for (let s = 0; s < segments; s++) {\n    const nx = cx + (Math.random() - 0.5) * jitter * 2;\n    const ny = cy + segDY * (0.8 + Math.random() * 0.4);\n    // Walk the segment with small steps \u2014 denser steps for a more\n    // solid-looking bolt.\n    const stepsPer = 8;\n    for (let i = 0; i < stepsPer; i++) {\n      const t = i / stepsPer;\n      const px = cx + (nx - cx) * t;\n      const py = cy + (ny - cy) * t;\n      if (py >= 0 && py < GH) {\n        // Small brush, very high strength so the bolt stays bright\n        // even after the rain washes over it.\n        paintAt(px, py, 1.5, 1, 0.65);\n      }\n    }\n    cx = nx;\n    cy = ny;\n  }\n}\n\nfunction thunderstormStep() {\n  // Rain phase\n  const spawnProb = STORM_SPAWN_PER_SEC / 60;\n  while (stormDrops.length < STORM_MAX_DROPS && Math.random() < spawnProb) {\n    stormDrops.push(stormSpawnDrop());\n  }\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  for (let i = stormDrops.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {\n    const d = stormDrops[i];\n    d.x += d.vx;\n    d.y += d.vy;\n    if (d.y > GH + 10 || d.x < -10 || d.x > GW + 10) { stormDrops.splice(i, 1); continue; }\n    if (d.y >= 0 && d.y < GH) {\n      paintAt(d.x, d.y, d.brushPx * pxToGridRadius, d.pigment, d.strength);\n    }\n  }\n  // Lightning phase \u2014 fires on cooldown, then resets timer to a randomized\n  // delay. Random cooldown spread makes flashes feel natural rather than\n  // metronomic.\n  stormLightningCooldown--;\n  if (stormLightningCooldown <= 0) {\n    stormFlashLightning();\n    stormLightningCooldown = STORM_LIGHTNING_MIN_COOLDOWN\n                           + Math.floor(Math.random() * STORM_LIGHTNING_RAND_COOLDOWN);\n  }\n}\n\n// ----- PARTLY CLOUDY preset --------------------------------------------\n// A few large slow soft \"clouds\" drift horizontally across the upper\n// half of the canvas. Each cloud paints multiple small dabs per frame\n// spread around its center, giving an irregular puffy footprint.\n// Water-brush dabs dominate so existing pigment gets lifted (reading as\n// brighter highlights); a minority of cerulean dabs adds shadow.\nconst CLOUD_MAX = 3;\n\nlet clouds = [];\nlet cloudSpawnTimer = 60;\n\nfunction cloudSpawn() {\n  const direction = Math.random() < 0.5 ? 1 : -1;\n  return {\n    x: direction > 0 ? -GW * 0.15 : GW * 1.15,\n    y: GH * (0.10 + Math.random() * 0.40),\n    vx: direction * (0.4 + Math.random() * 0.5),\n    halfWidth: GW * (0.10 + Math.random() * 0.10),    // half-extent for dab spread\n    halfHeight: GH * (0.04 + Math.random() * 0.04),\n    brushPx: 70 + Math.random() * 60,\n    life: 600,\n  };\n}\n\nfunction cloudsStep() {\n  cloudSpawnTimer--;\n  if (cloudSpawnTimer <= 0 && clouds.length < CLOUD_MAX) {\n    clouds.push(cloudSpawn());\n    cloudSpawnTimer = 180 + Math.floor(Math.random() * 240);\n  }\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  for (let i = clouds.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {\n    const c = clouds[i];\n    c.x += c.vx;\n    c.life--;\n    if (c.life <= 0 || c.x < -GW * 0.25 || c.x > GW * 1.25) { clouds.splice(i, 1); continue; }\n    // 2-3 dabs per frame, scattered within the cloud's footprint, so\n    // each cloud lays down a wide soft band rather than a thin trail.\n    const dabs = 2 + Math.floor(Math.random() * 2);\n    for (let j = 0; j < dabs; j++) {\n      const ox = (Math.random() - 0.5) * 2 * c.halfWidth;\n      const oy = (Math.random() - 0.5) * 2 * c.halfHeight;\n      // Mostly water (lifts to highlight), occasional cerulean for shadow.\n      const pig = Math.random() < 0.75 ? WATER_INDEX : 2;\n      paintAt(c.x + ox, c.y + oy, c.brushPx * pxToGridRadius, pig, 0.07);\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// ----- SNOWING preset --------------------------------------------------\n// Small slow drops with sinusoidal horizontal flutter \u2014 snowflakes\n// don't fall straight. The palette is cerulean-dominant at very low\n// strength: cerulean's granulation coefficient is high (\u03b3=0.75), so the\n// pigment settles into paper crevices and reads as a textured speckle,\n// which sells the snowflake look more than a smooth dab would.\nconst SNOW_MAX_DROPS = 16;\nconst SNOW_SPAWN_PER_SEC = 24;\n\nlet snowDrops = [];\n\nfunction snowSpawnDrop() {\n  return {\n    x: Math.random() * GW,\n    y: -10 - Math.random() * 30,\n    vy: 0.9 + Math.random() * 1.3,           // slow fall\n    flutterPhase: Math.random() * Math.PI * 2,\n    flutterAmp: 0.3 + Math.random() * 0.4,\n    flutterFreq: 0.04 + Math.random() * 0.04,\n    brushPx: 4 + Math.random() * 8,           // very small\n    pigment: Math.random() < 0.88 ? 2 : 0,    // mostly cerulean, occasional rose\n    strength: 0.10 + Math.random() * 0.10,\n  };\n}\n\nfunction snowingStep() {\n  const spawnProb = SNOW_SPAWN_PER_SEC / 60;\n  while (snowDrops.length < SNOW_MAX_DROPS && Math.random() < spawnProb) {\n    snowDrops.push(snowSpawnDrop());\n  }\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGridRadius = (GW / rect.width) * 0.5;\n  for (let i = snowDrops.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {\n    const d = snowDrops[i];\n    d.flutterPhase += d.flutterFreq;\n    d.x += Math.sin(d.flutterPhase) * d.flutterAmp;\n    d.y += d.vy;\n    if (d.y > GH + 10 || d.x < -10 || d.x > GW + 10) { snowDrops.splice(i, 1); continue; }\n    if (d.y >= 0 && d.y < GH) {\n      paintAt(d.x, d.y, d.brushPx * pxToGridRadius, d.pigment, d.strength);\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// ----- dispatcher -------------------------------------------------------\nfunction animationStep() {\n  switch (animationMode) {\n    case 'ai':           aiStep();           break;\n    case 'rainy':        rainStep();         break;\n    case 'sunny':        sunnyStep();        break;\n    case 'windy':        windyStep();        break;\n    case 'thunderstorm': thunderstormStep(); break;\n    case 'partlyCloudy': cloudsStep();       break;\n    case 'snowing':      snowingStep();      break;\n    // 'off' falls through to no-op\n  }\n}\n\nfunction clearAllAnimationState() {\n  aiState = null;\n  rainDrops = [];\n  sunnyStrokes = [];\n  sunnySpawnTimer = 30;\n  windyStreaks = [];\n  windDirection = 1;\n  stormDrops = [];\n  stormLightningCooldown = 120;\n  clouds = [];\n  cloudSpawnTimer = 60;\n  snowDrops = [];\n}\n\n// ----- UI: animation mode dropdown --------------------------------------\nconst animationSelect = document.getElementById('animation-mode');\nanimationSelect.addEventListener('change', () => {\n  // Discard every preset's in-flight state. Switching modes mid-stroke\n  // shouldn't leave half-fallen rain or a curving sun-sweep continuing\n  // into the new scene.\n  clearAllAnimationState();\n  animationMode = animationSelect.value;\n});",
